name: sustained_throughput_v1
description: Continuous-decode probe that runs each model for a fixed wall-clock window, cycling three long prompts. Measures whether short-burst throughput holds up under sustained pressure (thermal / power throttling).
notes: No quality scoring. The suite captures throughput, latency, and (when NVML is available) GPU temperature, power, and throttle reasons.
Three prompts are cycled to avoid pure cache effects.

Each prompt targets ~2000 output tokens; with max_tokens=512 the cycle naturally short-circuits at the sampling cap, which is the realistic Ollama default. Increase max_tokens in the experiment config for closer-to-spec runs.
Default duration is 300 seconds (5 minutes) per model; the spec's full 10-minute methodology is reachable via metadata.duration_seconds in the suite or duration_seconds in the runner call.

tasks.prompt: Explain how a transformer language model works in detail, starting from tokenization. Cover input embeddings, positional encoding, multi-head self-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer normalization, the role of softmax in producing logits, and how training optimizes the cross-entropy loss. Use clear paragraphs and worked examples where useful. Be thorough and aim for a long, careful answer.

Write a Python implementation of a B-tree with insert, delete, and search operations. Include docstrings for every public method, type hints, and inline comments where the invariants are non-obvious (split, merge, redistribute). Also include a small __main__ block that builds a tree, inserts several keys, deletes one, and prints the in-order traversal. Aim for a complete, runnable file.

Summarize the key arguments in the passage below and provide three substantive counterarguments. For each counterargument, identify the assumption it attacks, give a concrete example, and note what evidence would settle the dispute.

Passage: "Modern open-weight large language models have made closed-source frontier models almost economically irrational. The cost of running a 70B-class quantized model locally on a single high-bandwidth machine is now low enough that fine-tuning, prompt engineering, and dataset control outweigh the marginal capability gap. Within two years, most production inference will move on-premise, and the API era will be remembered as a brief commercial accident."
